---
title: "R Notebook"
output: html_notebook
---

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code. 

Try executing this chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Ctrl+Shift+Enter*. 

```{r}
plot(cars)
```

Add a new chunk by clicking the *Insert Chunk* button on the toolbar or by pressing *Ctrl+Alt+I*.

When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output will be saved alongside it (click the *Preview* button or press *Ctrl+Shift+K* to preview the HTML file).

The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the editor. Consequently, unlike *Knit*, *Preview* does not run any R code chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the editor is displayed.

<hr>

```
install.packages(ggplot2)
library(ggplot2)
```

## object in R

`R` rewrites objects without asking for permission or issuing a warning

```
object_name <- value
```

- `vector`: a sequence of data elements that are of the same type e.g. `integer`, `double`, `character`, `logical`, `complex` and `raw`

```{r}
int <- c(5,6,7,8)

# ensures the object will store integers instead of the default double

only_int <- c(5L,6L,7L,8L)

char <- c("R", "Programming")

logc <- c(FALSE, TRUE)

ls()

?is.complex()
?is.raw()

```

## Function

```
args(function.name) : returns the arguments a function takes
```

R has predefined argument order that's why the more arguments there are the higher the chance of getting the order wrong

## Simulation

```{r}
draw <- function(){
  deck <- c("king", "queen", "jack", "A")
  pick <- sample(deck,3,replace = T)
  print(pick)
}
```

## Matrix

> Built in function are weired in R because they can directly apply on object like `rownames(matrix_name)``

- Matrix are a natural extension to vector 
- They have a fixed number of rows and columns 
- Can contain only one basic data type

```{r}
gdp <- matrix(c(1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9),
              nrow=3,byrow = T, 
              dimnames = list(
                c("usa","ban","ind"),
                c("2018","2019","2020")
              ))
print(gdp)
```

## Categorical variable

The different categories in a categorical variables are called levels

```{r}
marital.status <- c("Married", "Married", "Single", "Married", "Divorced", "Single")
print(marital.status)
str(marital.status)
marital.factor <- factor(marital.status)
print(marital.factor)
```

## conditional statement

```{r}
z <- c(1,2,3,4)

if(z < 3){
  print("True at 3rd position")
}
```


## DataFrame

```{r}
title <- c("3 idiots", "CLOY", "GOBLIN")
rating <- c(9,6.5,8)
my.data <- data.frame(Movie_Name = title,Rating = rating)
print(my.data)
```


## Installing tidyverse

```
------------------------ ANTICONF ERROR ---------------------------
Configuration failed because libcurl was not found. Try installing:
 * deb: libcurl4-openssl-dev (Debian, Ubuntu, etc)
....
....
```

Copy and paste the following to your Terminal and than try installing tidyverse again. Should solve the problem.

```
# Required for many packages
sudo apt-get install -y libxml2-dev libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev
```

## Importing 

Read a file in table format and creates a data frame from it:

```
read.table("file.name", sep= , header= , stringsAsFactors=)
```

```{r}
my.pok <- read.table("pokRdex_comma.csv",
                     sep = ",",
                     header = T,
                     stringsAsFactors = F)

my.pok

```


Alternative function:

```
read.csv("file_name", stringsAsFactors= )
```

If the notation for the decimal is a comma then use `read.csv2()` or `read.delim2` function.

Getting a sense of your data:

- `nrows()`
- `ncols()`
- `colnames()`
- `rownames()`
- `str()`
- `summary()`

```
my.wars <- as.data.frame(starwars)
## my.wars[row,column]
head(my.wars[ , 1])
```

## Exportaing

Setting `row.names` to `FALSE` will save you unnecessary work 

```
write.csv(data, file="file_name.csv", row.names=FALSE)
```

## Manipulating data.frame

```
## adding columns 
data.frame$new.column
data.frame[["new.column"]]
cbind(data.frame, new.column)
## adding rows
rbind(data.frame,new.data.frame)
```

## Missing data

```{r}
example.na <- c(NA,1:10)
mean(example.na)
mean(example.na, na.rm = TRUE)
any(is.na(example.na))
example.na[is.na(example.na)] <- median(example.na, na.rm = TRUE)
example.na
```
